title: Polymer clay teardrop earrings
subtitle: A matched pair of teardrop-shaped earrings baked in a home oven
excerpt: Polymer clay teardrop earrings are cut from a rolled slab, textured or left plain, pierced for ear wires, and baked at 130°C for 20 minutes. The key to a matched pair is cutting both pieces from the same slab without re-rolling, so the clay texture and thickness are identical. No kiln is needed.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay earring technique. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: Making a matched pair of earrings in
is straightforward once you know the trick for matching: cut both pieces from the same rolled slab in a single session. Slab thickness and surface texture are then identical across the pair.
takes 20 minutes at 130°C. No kiln or metalworking skills are needed.

attrs.body: Ear wires (also called ear hooks or fish-hook earring wires) are available from craft suppliers in sterling silver, gold-fill, and surgical steel. Attach them to the baked and cooled earring through the pierced hole using jump rings. Do not bake ear wires in the clay.
attrs.title: Ear wires

content.text: Condition the clay until pliable. Roll to 3 mm thick on a smooth surface. Roll in multiple directions so the slab is even across its full area.

If a textured surface is wanted, press a stamp, leaf, or piece of lace into the slab now, before cutting. Apply even pressure across the whole slab so the texture depth is consistent between the two earrings.

Cut two identical teardrop shapes from adjacent areas of the slab using the craft knife. A teardrop about 4 cm tall and 2.5 cm wide at the widest point is a practical earring size. To get matching shapes, cut a paper template first and use it as a guide for both pieces.

Pierce a hole at the narrow tip of each teardrop using the needle tool, 2 mm from the tip. Pierce from front to back in a single movement. The hole should fit the jump ring wire, typically 1.5 to 2 mm.

Bake face-up on baking paper at 130°C for 20 minutes. Cool completely. Attach a jump ring through each hole and attach an ear wire to each jump ring. Seal with polymer clay varnish for a glossy finish, or leave matte.
Roll two colours together for a gradient effect before cutting the shapes.
Use a star or crescent cutter rather than a freehand teardrop for a more precise repeated shape.

attrs.intro: Problems when making polymer clay earrings.

items.fix: Make a paper template by tracing one shape and using it for the second cut. Both pieces should be cut from the same rolled slab in the same session.
items.cause: Cut freehand without a template, or from two separate rolled slabs.
items.symptom: Two earrings are noticeably different sizes

items.fix: Pierce the hole at least 2 mm from the tip. Ensure the oven is at 130°C (check with a thermometer) and bake for the full 20 minutes.
items.cause: Hole pierced too close to the edge, or clay not fully cured.
items.symptom: Hole at the tip cracks when the jump ring is inserted

attrs.heading: Common problems
